preamble.summary: A prayer of an afflicted person who pours out complaint before the LORD. The psalm moves through three movements: personal suffering described in vivid physical metaphors (vv. 1-12), a declaration that God will arise to rebuild Zion and respond to the destitute (vv. 13-23), and a meditation on human transience set against God's permanence (vv. 24-29). The superscription is unique in the Psalter — it describes a situation rather than naming an author.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 102 is one of the seven traditional penitential psalms, yet it contains no confession of sin. The sufferer is afflicted but not guilty — or at least the psalm never says so. The structural genius lies in how personal lament becomes national hope becomes cosmic theology. The psalmist begins with his own wasting body (bones burning, heart withered like grass, skin clinging to bones) and ends with God founding the heavens and the earth. The shift from 'my days are like a lengthening shadow' (v. 12) to 'You are the same, and your years have no end' (v. 28) is one of the most dramatic theological pivots in the Psalter. Human fragility is not denied but set within a framework of divine permanence. Hebrews 1:10-12 quotes verses 26-28 and applies them to the Son — making this psalm one of the key christological texts drawn from the Psalter.
preamble.friction: The superscription le-ani ki yaatof ('for the afflicted one when he is faint') does not name David or any other author, making this one of the few psalms with an entirely anonymous, situational heading. The transition from personal lament (vv. 1-12) to national restoration (vv. 13-23) is abrupt — some scholars argue the psalm combines two originally separate compositions. However, the logic is coherent if the psalmist identifies his own restoration with Zion's: when God rebuilds the city, He will also heal the sufferer. The individual and the nation share a single hope.
preamble.connections: The imagery of withered grass (v. 5, 12) connects to Isaiah 40:6-8 ('all flesh is grass'). The declaration that God founded the earth (v. 26) echoes Isaiah 48:13 and will be quoted in Hebrews 1:10-12 as testimony to Christ's eternal nature. The promise that God will 'look down from His holy height' (v. 20) to hear the groaning of prisoners echoes Psalm 79:11 and Exodus 2:23-25, where God heard the groaning of Israel in Egypt. The rebuilding of Zion (v. 17) connects to the post-exilic hope of Isaiah 60-62.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּפִלָּ֖ה לְעָנִ֥י כִֽי־יַעֲטֹ֑ף וְלִפְנֵ֥י יְ֝הוָ֗ה יִשְׁפֹּ֥ךְ שִׂיחֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear my prayer, O LORD, and let my cry come unto thee.
verses.rendering:
A prayer of the afflicted one, when he grows faint
and pours out his complaint before the LORD.
translator_notes: This verse is the superscription — not part of the prayer itself but a heading that describes the psalm's setting. The verb yaatof ('he grows faint, he is overwhelmed') conveys a person at the edge of endurance. The verb yishpokh ('he pours out') uses the language of liquid — the sufferer's complaint flows from him like water that cannot be held back. The noun sicho ('his complaint, his meditation') comes from siach, which can mean a spoken complaint or an inward brooding. This heading is unique in its specificity: it names no author, only a condition.

verses.text_hebrew: יְ֭הוָה שִׁמְעָ֣ה תְפִלָּתִ֑י וְ֝שַׁוְעָתִ֗י אֵלֶ֥יךָ תָבֽוֹא׃
verses.text_kjv: Hide not thy face from me in the day when I am in trouble; incline thine ear unto me: in the day when I call answer me speedily.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, hear my prayer,
and let my cry come before you.
translator_notes: The prayer proper begins with the standard petition formula: YHVH shim'ah tefillati ('LORD, hear my prayer'). The parallelism pairs tefillah ('prayer') with shav'ah ('cry for help') — the second term is more urgent, more desperate than the first. The verb tavo ('let it come') asks that the cry reach God's presence, implying a distance that must be crossed.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּסְתֵּ֥ר פָּנֶ֗יךָ מִ֭מֶּנִּי בְּי֣וֹם צַר־לִ֑י הַטֵּה־אֵלַ֥י אׇ֝זְנֶ֗ךָ בְּי֣וֹם אֶ֭קְרָא מַהֵ֥ר עֲנֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: For my days are consumed like smoke, and my bones are burned as an hearth.
verses.rendering:
Do not hide your face from me
in the day of my distress.
Incline your ear to me;
in the day I call, answer me quickly.
translator_notes: The phrase al taster panekha ('do not hide your face') is the foundational prayer of the Psalter — God's hidden face means abandonment, and God's turned face means salvation. The urgency intensifies through the verse: from 'hear' to 'do not hide' to 'incline your ear' to maher aneni ('answer me quickly'). The word maher ('quickly, speedily') expresses that the sufferer cannot wait — delay itself is a form of death.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־כָל֣וּ בְעָשָׁ֣ן יָמָ֑י וְ֝עַצְמוֹתַ֗י כְּמוֹ־קֵ֥ד נִחָֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: My heart is smitten, and withered like grass; so that I forget to eat my bread.
verses.rendering:
For my days vanish in smoke,
and my bones burn like a furnace.
verses.expanded_rendering: The body of the lament begins with two metaphors of consumption by fire. The phrase kalu ve-ashan yamai ('my days are consumed in smoke') means the psalmist's remaining life is dissipating like smoke from a fire — visible for a moment, then gone. The parallel line — ve-atsmotai kemo qed nicharu ('and my bones like a hearth are scorched') — moves from the external (days vanishing) to the internal (bones burning). The word qed refers to a hearth or firebrand, something that has been in the fire so long it is charred through. The bones are the deepest structure of the body, and they are on fire. This is not metaphor for mild discomfort — it is a person being consumed from the inside out.
translator_notes: The verb kalu (from kalah, 'to be finished, consumed, spent') indicates total depletion. The days are not merely passing; they are being used up, burned away. The word nicharu (from charar, 'to be scorched, to burn') describes dry, intense heat — the bones are desiccated by illness or suffering.

verses.text_hebrew: הוּכָּ֤ה כָעֵ֣שֶׂב וַיִּבַ֣שׁ לִבִּ֑י כִּֽי־שָׁ֝כַ֗חְתִּי מֵאֲכֹ֥ל לַחְמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: By reason of the voice of my groaning my bones cleave to my skin.
verses.rendering:
My heart is struck down and withered like grass,
for I forget to eat my bread.
translator_notes: The simile ka-esev ('like grass') invokes the Near Eastern image of grass that springs up green and is scorched by the sun within hours. The heart (lev) here is not the organ but the center of vitality and will. The connection between inner devastation and the forgetting of food (shakhachti me-ekhol lachmi) is psychologically precise — grief and suffering suppress appetite. The body refuses sustenance because the will to live has been struck.

verses.text_hebrew: מִקּ֥וֹל אַנְחָתִ֑י דָּבְקָ֥ה עַ֝צְמִ֗י לִבְשָׂרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I am like a pelican of the wilderness: I am like an owl of the desert.
verses.rendering:
From the sound of my groaning,
my bones cling to my flesh.
translator_notes: The verb daveqah ('it clings') is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 for marital union and in Psalm 101:3 for evil clinging. Here the clinging is skeletal — the bones press against the skin because there is no flesh between them. The person is wasting away. The phrase mi-qol anchati ('from the voice of my groaning') makes the groaning audible, the suffering public. The connection between sound and emaciation is causal: the groaning is so constant and intense that the body itself is being consumed by it.

verses.text_hebrew: דָּ֭מִיתִי לִקְאַ֣ת מִדְבָּ֑ר הָ֝יִ֗יתִי כְּכ֣וֹס חֳרָבֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: I watch, and am as a sparrow alone upon the house top.
verses.rendering:
I resemble a desert owl;
I have become like a screech owl among ruins.
translator_notes: The qa'at midbar ('pelican of the wilderness' in KJV) is more likely a type of owl or solitary bird associated with desolate places. The kos choravot ('owl of ruins, owl of waste places') is a bird that inhabits destroyed buildings and abandoned sites. Both images convey isolation in desolation — the psalmist is not merely alone but alone in a wasteland. The identification of these birds is uncertain; what matters is the theological resonance: both inhabit places where life has withdrawn.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁקַ֑דְתִּי וָאֶֽהְיֶ֗ה כְּצִפּ֥וֹר בּוֹדֵ֗ד עַל־גָּֽג׃
verses.text_kjv: Mine enemies reproach me all the day; and they that are mad against me are sworn against me.
verses.rendering:
I lie awake;
I am like a solitary bird on a rooftop.
translator_notes: The verb shaqadeti ('I keep watch, I lie awake') implies sleepless vigilance — the sufferer cannot rest. The image of a tsippor boded al gag ('a lonely bird on a roof') captures perfect isolation: a single bird perched on the highest point of a house, separated from its flock, exposed to wind and night. The rooftop is the most exposed part of an Israelite house. The bird is both visible and utterly alone.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הַ֭יּוֹם חֵרְפ֣וּנִי אוֹיְבָ֑י מְ֝הוֹלָלַ֗י בִּ֣י נִשְׁבָּֽעוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For I have eaten ashes like bread, and mingled my drink with weeping.
verses.rendering:
All day long my enemies taunt me;
those who mock me use my name as a curse.
translator_notes: The phrase bi nishba'u ('they swear by me') means the psalmist's misery has become proverbial — people use his name as an oath formula: 'May you become like him.' This is a depth of social humiliation beyond mere insult. The verb cherephuni ('they taunt me') implies public disgrace, and the meholalai ('those who rage against me, my mockers') are those who treat the sufferer as a spectacle of divine punishment.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־אֵ֣פֶר כַּלֶּ֣חֶם אָכָ֑לְתִּי וְ֝שִׁקֻּוַ֗י בִּבְכִ֥י מָסָֽכְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Because of thine indignation and thy wrath: for thou hast lifted me up, and cast me down.
verses.rendering:
For I eat ashes as my bread
and mingle my drink with weeping.
translator_notes: Ashes (epher) are the substance of mourning — the psalmist sits in ashes (like Job) and they have become his food. The image of mixing tears with drink (shiqquai bivkhi masakti) means weeping is so constant that tears literally fall into the cup. These are not separate metaphors but a portrait of a life in which grief has replaced every form of sustenance.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפְּנֵי־זַעַמְךָ֥ וְקִצְפֶּ֑ךָ כִּ֥י נְ֝שָׂאתַ֗נִי וַתַּשְׁלִיכֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: My days are like a shadow that declineth; and I am withered like grass.
verses.rendering:
Because of your indignation and your wrath,
for you lifted me up and hurled me down.
verses.expanded_rendering: The cause of suffering is now named: it is God. The phrase mippenei za'amkha ve-qitspekha ('because of your fury and your wrath') uses two words for divine anger — za'am (burning indignation) and qetseph (fierce wrath). The psalmist does not attribute his suffering to enemies or illness alone but to God's own hand. The image nesatani vattashlikeni ('you lifted me up and threw me down') is devastatingly physical — God picked the sufferer up like an object and hurled him to the ground. The lifting is not kindness but the prelude to a harder fall. The directness is characteristic of Hebrew prayer: the sufferer does not soften the accusation.
translator_notes: The verb nasah ('to lift up') followed by hishlikh ('to throw, to hurl') creates a single violent action in two stages. Some interpreters read this as a reference to former prosperity followed by catastrophic loss. The theological honesty is striking — the psalmist holds God responsible without abandoning prayer to God.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭מַי כְּצֵ֣ל נָט֑וּי וַ֝אֲנִ֗י כָּעֵ֥שֶׂב אִיבָֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou, O LORD, shalt endure for ever; and thy remembrance unto all generations.
verses.rendering:
My days are like a lengthening shadow,
and I wither like grass.
translator_notes: The tsel natui ('stretched-out shadow, declining shadow') is the shadow of late afternoon, growing longer as the sun descends — it will soon disappear entirely when night falls. The psalmist's remaining life stretches thin like that shadow. The return to the grass metaphor (ka-esev ivash, 'like grass I dry up') closes the personal lament section with the same image that opened it in verse 5. The ring structure emphasizes: the psalmist is dying.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּ֣ה יְ֭הוָה לְעוֹלָ֣ם תֵּשֵׁ֑ב וְ֝זִכְרְךָ֗ לְדֹ֣ר וָדֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt arise, and have mercy upon Zion: for the time to favour her, yea, the set time, is come.
verses.rendering:
But you, O LORD, are enthroned forever,
and your name endures to all generations.
verses.expanded_rendering: The great pivot of the psalm. The word ve-attah ('but you') is the hinge that turns everything. The psalmist has described his own disintegration — smoke, scorched bones, withered grass, lengthening shadows — and now sets all of it against God's permanence. The verb teshev ('you sit, you are enthroned') means God occupies His throne forever (le-olam). The psalmist fades; God remains. The phrase zikhrekha le-dor va-dor ('your remembrance to generation and generation') means God's name and reputation endure across all human time. The contrast is not meant to diminish the sufferer but to anchor hope: if God is permanent, then the permanent God can act to save the perishing sufferer.
translator_notes: The phrase le-olam teshev ('forever you sit enthroned') uses olam in its fullest sense — perpetual, without end. The contrast between the psalmist's shadow-days and God's endless reign is the theological engine that drives the rest of the psalm toward hope.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֣ה תָ֭קוּם תְּרַחֵ֣ם צִיּ֑וֹן כִּי־עֵ֥ת לְ֝חֶנְנָ֗הּ כִּי־בָ֥א מוֹעֵֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: For thy servants take pleasure in her stones, and favour the dust thereof.
verses.rendering:
You will rise and have compassion on Zion,
for it is time to show her favor —
the appointed time has come.
translator_notes: The verb taqum ('you will arise') implies that God has been seated, possibly in judgment or in apparent inaction, and now rises to act. The verb terachem ('you will have compassion') uses the root rachum, connected to rechem ('womb') — God's compassion is maternal, visceral, womb-deep. The phrase et lechennah ('time to show her grace') and ba mo'ed ('the appointed time has come') assert that there is a divinely fixed moment for Zion's restoration, and it has arrived. The psalmist's personal suffering is now linked to national hope.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־רָצ֣וּ עֲ֭בָדֶיךָ אֶת־אֲבָנֶ֑יהָ וְאֶת־עֲפָרָ֥הּ יְחֹנֵֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: So the heathen shall fear the name of the LORD, and all the kings of the earth thy glory.
verses.rendering:
For your servants hold dear her stones
and look with compassion on her dust.
translator_notes: The verb ratsu ('they take pleasure in, they hold dear') means the people love even the rubble of Zion — her stones (avaneiha) and her dust (apharah). This is post-destruction language: the city has been reduced to ruins, but its broken stones are precious to the faithful. The word aphar ('dust') is the material of desolation and also of creation (Genesis 2:7). Even Zion's dust is beloved. This verse likely reflects the exile or early post-exilic period.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִירְא֣וּ ג֭וֹיִם אֶת־שֵׁ֣ם יְהוָ֑ה וְכׇל־מַלְכֵ֥י הָ֝אָ֗רֶץ אֶת־כְּבוֹדֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: When the LORD shall build up Zion, he shall appear in his glory.
verses.rendering:
Then the nations will fear the name of the LORD,
and all the kings of the earth your glory.
translator_notes: The scope expands from Zion to the entire world. When God rebuilds Zion, the nations (goyim) will respond with yir'ah ('fear, reverence'). The parallel between shem YHVH ('the name of the LORD') and kevodekha ('your glory') equates God's name with His manifest presence. The kings of the earth — not merely Israel's neighbors but all earthly rulers — will acknowledge God's kavod.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, heaviness, splendor, radiance, the manifest presence of God
key_terms.note: kavod derives from the root k-v-d ('to be heavy, weighty'). God's kavod is His perceptible presence — when it appears, it cannot be ignored. Here the rebuilding of Zion will make God's glory visible to the nations.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־בָנָ֣ה יְהוָ֣ה צִיּ֑וֹן נִ֝רְאָ֗ה בִּכְבוֹדֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He will regard the prayer of the destitute, and not despise their prayer.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD will rebuild Zion;
He will appear in His glory.
translator_notes: The verb banah ('to build') is the hope of every exile — God will reconstruct what was destroyed. The phrase nir'ah bikhvodo ('He will be seen in His glory') means God's rebuilding of Zion is not merely architectural but theophanic — God Himself will appear. The city is rebuilt because God returns to it.

verses.text_hebrew: פָּ֭נָה אֶל־תְּפִלַּ֣ת הָעַרְעָ֑ר וְלֹא־בָ֝זָ֗ה אֶת־תְּפִלָּתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: This shall be written for the generation to come: and the people which shall be created shall praise the LORD.
verses.rendering:
He has turned toward the prayer of the destitute
and has not despised their plea.
translator_notes: The ha-ar'ar ('the stripped one, the destitute, the naked shrub') is a rare word — it appears in Jeremiah 17:6 as a bush in the desert, stripped bare, without hope. Here it describes people stripped of everything. The verb bazah ('to despise') is what God does not do — He does not look down on the prayer of those who have nothing. This verse reverses the shame the psalmist described earlier: even if enemies despise him, God does not.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּכָּ֣תֶב זֹ֖את לְד֣וֹר אַחֲר֑וֹן וְעַ֥ם נִ֝בְרָ֗א יְהַלֶּל־יָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For he hath looked down from the height of his sanctuary; from heaven did the LORD behold the earth;
verses.rendering:
Let this be written for a generation to come,
that a people yet to be created may praise the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist calls for his testimony to be recorded — tikkattev zot ('let this be written') — so that future generations can read it and praise God. The phrase am nivra ('a people yet created') is extraordinary: the psalmist is writing for people who do not yet exist. The verb nivra (Niphal of bara, 'to create') echoes Genesis 1 — God creates peoples as He creates worlds. The purpose of the testimony is yehalel Yah ('he will praise the LORD'). The suffering, the hope, and the restoration are all recorded so that praise can continue across time. The psalmist writes himself into the future.
translator_notes: The phrase dor acharon ('a later generation, a generation to come') combined with am nivra ('a people to be created') gives this verse an almost prophetic quality — the psalm reaches beyond its own moment to address readers who have not yet been born. Every subsequent reader of this psalm fulfills the psalmist's hope.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־הִ֭שְׁקִיף מִמְּר֣וֹם קׇדְשׁ֑וֹ יְהוָ֓ה ׀ מִשָּׁמַ֖יִם אֶל־אֶ֣רֶץ הִבִּֽיט׃
verses.text_kjv: To hear the groaning of the prisoner; to loose those that are appointed to death;
verses.rendering:
For He looked down from His holy height;
the LORD gazed from heaven to earth,
translator_notes: The verb hishqif ('He looked down, He gazed from above') implies bending forward to see — an act of deliberate attention. The phrase mi-merom qodsho ('from the height of His holiness') locates God in the highest point of the cosmic architecture, yet He bends to look at the earth. The verbs hishqif and hibbit ('He looked intently') both emphasize focused, purposeful observation — God is not passively aware but actively watching.

verses.text_hebrew: לִ֭שְׁמֹעַ אֶנְקַ֣ת אָסִ֑יר לְ֝פַתֵּ֗חַ בְּנֵ֣י תְמוּתָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: To declare the name of the LORD in Zion, and his praise in Jerusalem;
verses.rendering:
to hear the groaning of the prisoner,
to set free those condemned to death,
translator_notes: The enqat asir ('groaning of the prisoner') echoes Exodus 2:24 and 6:5, where God hears the groaning of Israel in Egypt. The phrase benei temutah ('sons of death, children of death') means those sentenced to die — death is their parentage, their destiny. God's purpose in looking down is not idle surveillance but rescue: lefatte'ach ('to open, to release'). The God who gazes from heaven does so in order to act.

verses.text_hebrew: לְסַפֵּ֣ר בְּ֭צִיּוֹן שֵׁ֣ם יְהוָ֑ה וּ֝תְהִלָּת֗וֹ בִּירוּשָׁלָֽ͏ִם׃
verses.text_kjv: When the people are gathered together, and the kingdoms, to serve the LORD.
verses.rendering:
that the name of the LORD may be proclaimed in Zion
and His praise in Jerusalem,
translator_notes: The verb lesapper ('to recount, to proclaim, to tell') is the purpose of the entire rescue: God frees the prisoners so that they can tell what He has done. The parallel between shem YHVH ('the name of the LORD') in Zion and tehillato ('His praise') in Jerusalem binds proclamation to place — the restored city is the location where God's acts are celebrated.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּהִקָּבֵ֣ץ עַמִּ֣ים יַחְדָּ֑ו וּ֝מַמְלָכ֗וֹת לַעֲבֹ֥ד אֶת־יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He weakened my strength in the way; he shortened my days.
verses.rendering:
when peoples gather together,
and kingdoms, to serve the LORD.
translator_notes: The vision expands to eschatological scope: be-hiqqavets ammim yachdav ('when peoples are gathered together') — not just Israel but the nations. The word mamlakhot ('kingdoms') pairs with ammim ('peoples') to indicate both populations and political structures. The purpose of this universal gathering is la'avod et YHVH ('to serve the LORD'). The psalm has moved from one sufferer's bones on fire to all the kingdoms of the earth worshipping God. The trajectory is from desolation to cosmic restoration.

verses.text_hebrew: עִנָּ֣ה בַדֶּ֣רֶךְ כֹּחִ֑י קִ֝צַּ֗ר יָמָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I said, O my God, take me not away in the midst of my days: thy years are throughout all generations.
verses.rendering:
He has broken my strength along the way;
He has cut short my days.
translator_notes: The psalmist returns to personal lament. The verb innah ('He afflicted, He humbled') attributes the suffering directly to God — not to enemies, not to illness, but to the divine hand. The phrase badderekh ('along the way, on the road') implies the sufferer was cut down mid-journey, before reaching the destination. The verb qittsar ('He shortened') means God truncated the psalmist's life — the full measure of days was not granted.

verses.text_hebrew: אֹ֭מַר אֵלִ֗י אַל־תַּעֲלֵ֥נִי בַחֲצִ֗י יָ֫מָ֥י בְּד֖וֹר דּוֹרִ֥ים שְׁנוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Of old hast thou laid the foundation of the earth: and the heavens are the work of thy hands.
verses.rendering:
I say, 'My God, do not take me away
in the middle of my days.'
Your years span all generations.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist pleads: al ta'aleni bachasi yamai ('do not bring me up in the half of my days') — do not take me at the midpoint of my life, before my time is complete. The verb ta'aleni ('bring me up, take me away') is a euphemism for death. But the plea is immediately set against a contrasting truth: be-dor dorim shenotekha ('your years are through all generations'). The juxtaposition is the psalm's central theological tension: the psalmist's days are cut short; God's years never end. The sufferer's prayer for more time is spoken before the One who has all time.
translator_notes: The contrast between 'the middle of my days' and 'your years throughout all generations' is the theological hinge of the psalm's final section. Human life is measured in halves and portions; God's existence is measured in all generations. The psalmist does not resolve this tension — he holds both truths together.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭פָנִים הָאָ֣רֶץ יָסַ֑דְתָּ וּֽמַעֲשֵׂ֖ה יָדֶ֣יךָ שָׁמָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: They shall perish, but thou shalt endure: yea, all of them shall wax old like a garment; as a vesture shalt thou change them, and they shall be changed:
verses.rendering:
Long ago you laid the foundation of the earth,
and the heavens are the work of your hands.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now reaches its cosmic register. The word lefanim ('long ago, formerly, in the beginning') points back to the origin of everything. The verb yasadta ('you founded, you laid the foundation') uses the language of architecture — God laid the earth's foundation the way a builder sets a cornerstone. The parallel line — uma'aseh yadekha shamayim ('and the work of your hands are the heavens') — attributes the sky itself to God's craftsmanship. Hands (yadekha) are emphasized: the heavens are handmade by God. This verse is quoted in Hebrews 1:10, where the author applies it to the Son, arguing that the One who made heaven and earth is the same One who entered it.
translator_notes: The verb yasad ('to found, to establish, to lay a foundation') is used for both the temple and the earth — both are structures God has built. The psalm does not distinguish between creation and architecture; for the Hebrew mind, the cosmos is God's building project.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֤מָּה ׀ יֹאבֵ֗דוּ וְאַתָּ֣ה תַ֭עֲמֹד וְכֻלָּ֗ם כַּבֶּ֣גֶד יִבְל֑וּ כַּלְּב֖וּשׁ תַּחֲלִיפֵ֣ם וְיַחֲלֹֽפוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou art the same, and thy years shall have no end.
verses.expanded_rendering: The heavens and the earth — the very things God made — will perish (yovedu). But God will stand (ta'amod). The verb amad ('to stand') contrasts with the verb avad ('to perish'): creation is temporary; the Creator is permanent. The metaphor that follows is stunning: ve-khullam kabbaged yivlu ('all of them will wear out like a garment'). The cosmos is clothing that God wears — and clothing wears out. The verb tachaliphem ('you will change them') means God will swap out the heavens like a person changes clothes: kallevush tachaliphem ve-yachalofu ('like a garment you will change them and they will pass away'). The universe is God's wardrobe, and He is free to replace it.
translator_notes: The garment metaphor is quoted in Hebrews 1:11-12 and applied to the Son's superiority over creation. The theological weight is enormous: if even the heavens wear out, then nothing created is permanent — only the Creator endures. The verb chalaf ('to pass away, to change') applies to the heavens what normally applies to human generations.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּה־ה֑וּא וּ֝שְׁנוֹתֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א יִתָּֽמּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The children of thy servants shall continue, and their seed shall be established before thee.
verses.rendering:
But you are the same,
and your years will never end.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase ve-attah hu ('but you — He') is one of the most compressed theological statements in the Hebrew Bible. The pronoun hu ('He') functions almost as a divine name — 'You are He,' meaning 'You are the One who is, the unchanging One.' The Septuagint translates this as su de ho autos ei ('but you are the same'), and Hebrews 1:12 follows this reading. God's identity does not change. His years (shenotekha) will not end (lo yittammu, from tamam, 'to be complete, to be finished'). Everything else runs out; God does not. The psalmist who feared being taken away in the middle of his days now rests his hope on the One whose days have no middle and no end.
translator_notes: The phrase attah hu ('you are He') became a liturgical formula in later Judaism. It affirms God's unchanging nature — He is the same yesterday, today, and forever, as Hebrews 13:8 will later say of Christ. The psalm's movement from personal suffering to cosmic permanence is complete.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י עֲ֭בָדֶיךָ יִשְׁכּ֑וֹנוּ וְ֝זַרְעָ֗ם לְפָנֶ֥יךָ יִכּֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: The children of thy servants shall continue, and their seed shall be established before thee.
verses.rendering:
The children of your servants will dwell secure,
and their offspring will be established before you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends not with the psalmist's own survival but with the future of God's servants' children. The verb yishkonu ('they will dwell, they will settle') promises stability and rest. The phrase zar'am lefanekha yikkon ('their seed before you will be established') uses the verb kun ('to be firm, to be established') — the same stability that belongs to God's throne now extends to the descendants of the faithful. The psalm has moved from a single sufferer's ashes and tears to the enduring security of God's people across generations. The answer to human fragility is not human permanence — it is divine permanence that shelters human generations within itself.
translator_notes: The word zera ('seed, offspring') is the covenant term for descendants — the promise to Abraham that his seed would endure. The psalm closes by grounding individual hope in communal continuity: the sufferer may die, but the community of God's servants will persist before God's face.
